content.text: Kill Switches for Agent Behaviors in Build

An AI agent that can send emails, write to a database, or call external APIs represents a fundamentally different risk profile from a feature that renders text differently. If the agent generates a hallucinated recipient list and tries to send a thousand emails, a feature flag at the UI layer will not stop it — the action happens inside the agent loop before any user sees output. Kill switches for agent behaviors must be embedded in the agent code itself, checked before every consequential action.

This sub-subtopic covers how to design and wire hierarchical kill switches into agent code from the start of the build phase, so that any behavior can be disabled at any granularity — globally, per-agent, or per-tool — without a code deploy.

attrs.alt: Hierarchical kill switch structure: global → agent → tool level with resolution logic

content.text: Why Agent Behaviors Need Their Own Kill Switches

Standard feature flags answer one question: should this user see the AI feature? Agent kill switches answer a finer question: should this agent be allowed to execute this specific action right now? The distinction matters because agents compose multiple tools, and only some of those tools may need to be paused.
Dimension
Feature Flag
Agent Kill Switch
Scope
Controls whether feature is visible to a user
Controls whether an action is executed by the agent
Granularity
Binary on/off per feature
Global / per-agent / per-tool / per-action-type
Timing
Checked at request routing layer
Checked immediately before each consequential action
Effect
User sees fallback UI
Agent receives ToolDisabledError; handles gracefully
Urgency
Minutes acceptable — UI only
Sub-second required — prevents irreversible actions
Kill Switch Schema

Define kill switch state as a structured schema in the flag store. Three levels — global, agent, and tool — each independently toggleable. Store in Redis with a short TTL so the agent always reads a fresh value without long-polling:
Checking Kill Switches Before Tool Execution

The kill switch check belongs inside the tool executor, not the agent orchestration layer. This ensures the check happens regardless of which agent loop invokes the tool, and that it cannot be bypassed by a new orchestrator that forgets to add the check:
Applying a Kill Switch at Runtime
An operator applies a kill switch by writing to the Redis key directly or via a CLI. The agent reads the updated state within the TTL window (10 seconds), no restart required:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# agents/cli/set_kill_switch.py — apply or clear a kill switch
import argparse, json, os
import redis

def set_kill_switch(
    redis_url: str,
    global_enabled: bool | None,
    agent_id: str | None,
    agent_enabled: bool | None,
    tool_name: str | None,
    tool_enabled: bool | None,
) -> None:
    r = redis.from_url(redis_url)
    raw = r.get("agent_kill_switches")
    data = json.loads(raw) if raw else {
        "global_enabled": True, "agents": {}, "tools": {}
    }

    if global_enabled is not None:
        data["global_enabled"] = global_enabled
    if agent_id is not None and agent_enabled is not None:
        data["agents"][agent_id] = agent_enabled
    if tool_name is not None and tool_enabled is not None:
        data["tools"][tool_name] = tool_enabled

    r.set("agent_kill_switches", json.dumps(data))
    print("Kill switch state updated:")
    print(json.dumps(data, indent=2))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--redis-url", default=os.environ["PROD_REDIS_URL"])
    parser.add_argument("--global-enabled", type=lambda v: v.lower() != "false")
    parser.add_argument("--agent-id")
    parser.add_argument("--agent-enabled", type=lambda v: v.lower() != "false")
    parser.add_argument("--tool-name")
    parser.add_argument("--tool-enabled", type=lambda v: v.lower() != "false")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    set_kill_switch(
        redis_url=args.redis_url,
        global_enabled=args.global_enabled,
        agent_id=args.agent_id,
        agent_enabled=args.agent_enabled,
        tool_name=args.tool_name,
        tool_enabled=args.tool_enabled,
    )

# Example: disable just the write_db tool across all agents
# python3 agents/cli/set_kill_switch.py --tool-name write_db --tool-enabled false
Graceful Degradation vs Hard Stop
When a kill switch fires, the agent must decide between two responses:
hard stop
(raise an error, abort the entire task) or
graceful degradation
(skip the disabled action, return a partial result, notify the caller). The right choice depends on whether the disabled tool is essential to the task:
Scenario
Approach
Example Response
Core action disabled (tool is the whole point)
Hard stop
raise ToolDisabledError — let the caller see "unavailable"
Side-effect tool disabled (logging, caching, analytics)
Graceful skip
Log the skip, continue without the side effect
One of several retrieval tools disabled
Fallback
Use alternate retrieval tool; note in response metadata
Global kill switch or agent-level kill switch
Hard stop
Return "agent temporarily unavailable" to all callers
Unit Testing Kill Switch Coverage
Every agent must have unit tests that verify both the enabled path and the disabled path for each kill switch level. Tests should use an in-memory
KillSwitchStore
stub that does not call Redis:

# tests/agents/test_email_agent_kill_switches.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock
from agents.kill_switches import AgentKillSwitchState, KillSwitchStore
from agents.tool_executor import ToolExecutor, ToolDisabledError
from agents.email_agent import EmailAgent

def make_executor(state: AgentKillSwitchState, agent_id: str) -> ToolExecutor:
    store = MagicMock(spec=KillSwitchStore)
    store.get.return_value = state
    return ToolExecutor(ks_store=store, agent_id=agent_id)


class TestEmailAgentKillSwitches:
    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_enabled_path_sends_email(self):
        state = AgentKillSwitchState(global_enabled=True)
        executor = make_executor(state, "email_agent")
        agent = EmailAgent(executor=executor)
        result = await agent.run("Send hello to alice@example.com")
        assert "sent" in result.lower()

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_global_kill_switch_prevents_send(self):
        state = AgentKillSwitchState(global_enabled=False)
        executor = make_executor(state, "email_agent")
        agent = EmailAgent(executor=executor)
        result = await agent.run("Send hello to alice@example.com")
        assert "unavailable" in result.lower()
        # Verify no actual send occurred
        assert "sent" not in result.lower()

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_agent_level_kill_switch(self):
        state = AgentKillSwitchState(
            global_enabled=True,
            agents={"email_agent": False},
        )
        executor = make_executor(state, "email_agent")
        agent = EmailAgent(executor=executor)
        result = await agent.run("Send hello to alice@example.com")
        assert "unavailable" in result.lower()

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_tool_level_kill_switch_send_email(self):
        state = AgentKillSwitchState(
            global_enabled=True,
            agents={},
            tools={"send_email": False},
        )
        executor = make_executor(state, "email_agent")
        agent = EmailAgent(executor=executor)
        result = await agent.run("Send hello to alice@example.com")
        assert "unavailable" in result.lower()

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_unrelated_tool_kill_switch_does_not_affect_agent(self):
        state = AgentKillSwitchState(
            global_enabled=True,
            tools={"write_db": False},  # different agent's tool
        )
        executor = make_executor(state, "email_agent")
        agent = EmailAgent(executor=executor)
        result = await agent.run("Send hello to alice@example.com")
        assert "sent" in result.lower()
Observability: Logging Kill Switch Events

Every kill switch trigger must be logged with full context: which agent, which tool, which kill switch level fired, and a request trace ID. These logs are the primary signal during an incident — they confirm that the kill switch is actually protecting against actions, and they help the on-call engineer understand the blast radius of what was stopped.

# agents/tool_executor.py — add structured logging to the executor
import structlog
from agents.kill_switches import KillSwitchStore, AgentKillSwitchState
from agents.tool_executor import ToolDisabledError

log = structlog.get_logger(__name__)

class ObservableToolExecutor:
    def __init__(self, ks_store: KillSwitchStore, agent_id: str, trace_id: str) -> None:
        self._ks = ks_store
        self._agent_id = agent_id
        self._trace_id = trace_id

    def execute(self, tool_name: str, fn, **kwargs):
        ks = self._ks.get()
        ctx = {"agent_id": self._agent_id, "tool": tool_name, "trace_id": self._trace_id}

        if not ks.global_enabled:
            log.warning("kill_switch.global_fired", **ctx, level="global")
            raise ToolDisabledError(self._agent_id, tool_name, "global kill switch")

        if not ks.agent_enabled(self._agent_id):
            log.warning("kill_switch.agent_fired", **ctx, level="agent")
            raise ToolDisabledError(self._agent_id, tool_name,
                f"agent '{self._agent_id}' disabled")

        if not ks.tool_enabled(tool_name):
            log.warning("kill_switch.tool_fired", **ctx, level="tool")
            raise ToolDisabledError(self._agent_id, tool_name,
                f"tool '{tool_name}' disabled")

        log.debug("tool.executing", **ctx)
        result = fn(**kwargs)
        log.debug("tool.completed", **ctx)
        return result

# Dashboard alert rule (pseudo-DSL):
#
#   ALERT AgentKillSwitchFired
#   WHEN rate(kill_switch_global_fired[1m]) > 0
#      OR rate(kill_switch_agent_fired[1m]) > 5
#   FOR 1m
#   NOTIFY oncall-agents-team
#   MESSAGE "Kill switch active — check agent_id and tool fields"
#
# A single global kill switch event in production is always worth an alert.
# Agent-level events at low rate are expected during rolling deployments.
Wiring Kill Switches into the Build Pipeline
Kill switch checks should fail a build fast if the code forgets to use the executor. Add a linter rule that rejects any agent method that calls an external tool function directly, bypassing
ToolExecutor.execute()
. This prevents the class of bug where a new tool is added but the kill switch check is omitted:
Summary
Kill switches for agent behaviors are not feature flags at the UI layer — they are checked inside the agent loop, before every consequential action, and they resolve hierarchically:
global → agent → tool
. Build them into the
ToolExecutor

from day one so the check cannot be omitted by a new agent implementation. Keep the kill switch state in Redis with a short TTL (10s) so that an operator can halt a misbehaving agent's actions within seconds, without a code deploy or container restart.
Design every agent to handle
ToolDisabledError

explicitly: hard-stop for essential actions, graceful-skip for side-effect tools. Test both the enabled and disabled path for each kill switch level — a kill switch that works but causes the agent to crash is worse than useless when you need it most in production.
